title: Caller-supplied workerUrl is passed to Worker() without origin or allowlist validation
description: spawnWorker() accepts an arbitrary workerUrl string and passes it directly to new Worker(url, { type: 'module' }). A caller (or any code that controls the spawn call) can point the URL at a cross-origin attacker-controlled script, causing the worker to execute arbitrary code with the same origin-permissions as the parent page. In practice this is only exploitable when caller input flows from user-controlled data (e.g. a URL query param surfaced to jxl-session spawn opts), but there is no defensive check. The fix is to validate that the URL is same-origin before constructing the Worker, or document that the caller is solely responsible.

title: Full internal error strings from WASM load failures are forwarded to the main thread
description: When WASM module loading fails, the raw exception message (including stack fragments from dynamic import failures) is embedded verbatim in a decode_error / encode_error protocol message and sent to the main thread: `WASM module failed to load: ${String(err)}`. In a production environment these strings may include local file system paths, package versions, or internal stack traces that help an attacker fingerprint the deployment. The same pattern appears in the catch handler for unexpected session start errors (line 418). The fix is to log the full error internally and forward only a sanitized, user-facing code string.

title: Raw wasmUrl (caller-supplied) reflected back in thrown error message
description: loadWasmModule() embeds the raw wasmUrl argument in the thrown Error message: `WASM not available at ${wasmUrl} (${probeStatus})`. This string propagates to the main thread via the worker catch handler (worker.ts line 384) and then into decode_error.message visible to the application. If wasmUrl originates from a configuration value that embeds credentials or internal hostnames (e.g. a signed S3 URL or a corporate CDN path), those values are disclosed to any consumer that logs error messages. The fix is to strip query strings and credentials from the URL before including it in the user-facing error.

title: Dimension overflow check uses width*height which can silently truncate for very large integer inputs before comparison
description: The overflow guard computes `width * height` in JavaScript's 64-bit float arithmetic. For width and height values approaching 2^53 (which a crafted codestream with unchecked integer parsing could supply), the multiplication overflows float precision before it reaches Infinity, producing a result that compares as less than MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES_GUARD (1 GiB) despite representing a physically impossible allocation. For example, width=1_073_741_824 (2^30) and height=1_073_741_824 would multiply to exactly 2^60 = 1152921504606846976, which is not Infinity and far exceeds the 1 GiB guard — meaning the guard would pass. The check should additionally validate that each individual dimension is finite and below a reasonable per-axis cap (e.g. 65535 or 131072) before multiplying.

title: Diagnostic probe fetch uses the caller-supplied wasmUrl without restricting the URL scheme or host
description: When the dynamic import succeeds but the module lacks createDecoder/createEncoder, loadWasmModule() falls through to a diagnostic HEAD fetch against wasmUrl. Because wasmUrl is caller-supplied and not validated against a same-origin or allowlisted-scheme requirement, a crafted wasmUrl (e.g. `http://internal-metadata-service/` in a cloud deployment) causes the worker to emit an outbound request to an attacker-controlled or internal endpoint. The probe result is only used to improve error messaging, making its primary value diagnostic, not functional — it should be gated on the URL being same-origin or should be removed entirely.

title: Incoming worker messages are not structurally validated before dispatch
description: The onmessage router trusts the shape of every MainToWorkerMessage entirely — it dispatches on msg.type and reads msg.sessionId, msg.chunk, msg.region etc. without any runtime schema check. A malformed message (e.g. one with a numeric sessionId, a negative byteLength chunk, or a region with non-integer coordinates) reaches handler logic and WASM FFI without validation. Since the worker runs in a browser context where any same-origin page can postMessage to a named worker port, this is a meaningful attack surface. Adding a lightweight runtime validation (discriminated-union type guards on each message type) at the router boundary would catch malformed messages early.

category: missing origin check on worker messages
title: Worker message handler does not check MessageEvent.origin before processing
description: The MessageEvent.origin field is not inspected before dispatching messages. While DedicatedWorker postMessage only accepts messages from its owning context in current browsers, SharedWorker and ServiceWorker contexts do not have this guarantee, and browser bugs or future spec evolution could change this. Additionally, in environments where the worker script is reused across multiple page contexts (e.g. transferred via MessageChannel), an unexpected sender could trigger session state manipulation. Checking that event.origin matches the expected origin adds a defense-in-depth guard at negligible cost.

title: abortedStarts Set grows unboundedly — no eviction for completed sessions
description: Session IDs are added to abortedStarts when a start is cancelled before the WASM loads, and are removed only by abortedStarts.delete() during the successful (non-aborted) start path. If a caller rapidly creates and aborts sessions with unique IDs (which are strings and thus unconstrained), abortedStarts can grow without bound, gradually consuming worker memory. Over a long-lived worker lifespan this is a slow memory leak. The fix is to add an entry to the regular session cleanup path (handleReleaseState + finishSession) to ensure stale abort records are reaped.
